question:
Let $\mathcal{A}$
be a non-empty set of positive integers, and let $N(x)$ denote
the number of elements of $\mathcal{A}$ not exceeding $x$.
Let $\mathcal{B}$ denote the set
of positive integers $b$ that can be written in the form $b = a - a'$ with
$a \in \mathcal{A}$  and $a' \in  \mathcal{A}$. Let $b_1 < b_2 < \cdots$
be the members of $\mathcal{B}$,
listed in increasing order. Show that if the sequence $b_{i+1} - b_i$ is
unbounded, then
\[
\lim_{x \to\infty}  N(x)/x  = 0.
\]

\end{itemize}
\end{document}
solution:
\textbf{First solution:}
(based on a solution of Dan Bernstein)
Note that for any $b$, the condition that $b \notin \calB$ already
forces $\limsup N(x)/x$ to be at most 1/2: pair off $2mb + n$ with
$(2m+1)b+n$ for $n=1,\dots, b$, and note that at most one member of each
pair may belong to $\calA$.
The idea of the proof is to do something
similar with pairs replaced by larger clumps, using long runs of
excluded elements of $B$.

Suppose we have positive integers
$b_0 = 1, b_1, \dots, b_n$ with
the following properties:
\begin{enumerate}
\item[(a)] For $i=1, \dots, n$,
$c_i = b_i/(2b_{i-1})$ is an integer.
\item[(b)] For $e_i \in \{-1,0,1\}$,
$|e_1b_1 + \cdots + e_n b_n| \notin \calB$.
\end{enumerate}
Each nonnegative integer $a$ has a unique ``base expansion''
\[
a = a_0 b_0 + \cdots + a_{n-1} b_{n-1} + m b_n \qquad (0 \leq a_i < 2c_i);
\]
if two integers have expansions with the same value of $m$, and values
of $a_i$ differing by at most 1 for $i=0, \dots, n-1$, then their
difference is not in $\calB$, so at most one of them lies in $\calA$.
In particular,
for any $d_i \in \{0, \dots, c_i - 1\}$, any $m_0 \in \{0, 2c_0 - 1\}$
and any $m_n$, the set
\begin{multline*}
\{m_0 b_0 + (2d_1 +e_1)b_0 + \cdots \\
+ (2d_{n-1} + e_{n-1})b_{n-1} + (2m_n + e_n)b_n\},
\end{multline*}
where each $e_i$ runs over $\{0,1\}$,
contains at most one element of $\calA$; consequently,
$\limsup N(x)/x \leq 1/2^{n}$.

We now produce such $b_i$ recursively, starting with $b_0 = 1$ (and both
(a) and (b) holding vacuously).  Given $b_0, \dots, b_n$ satisfying (a)
and (b), note that $b_0 + \cdots + b_{n-1} < b_n$ by induction on $n$.
By the hypotheses of the problem, we can find a set $S_n$ of $6b_n$
consecutive integers, none of which belongs to $\calB$. Let $b_{n+1}$
be the second-smallest multiple of $2b_n$ in $S_n$; then $b_{n+1} + x \in
S_n$ for $-2b_n \leq x \leq 0$ clearly, and also for $0 \leq x \leq 2b_n$
because there are most $4b_n-1$ elements of $S_n$ preceding $b_{n+1}$.
In particular, the analogue of (b) with $n$ replaced by $n+1$ holds for
$e_{n+1} \neq 0$; of course it holds for $e_{n+1} = 0$ because (b) was
already known. Since the analogue of (a) holds by construction, we have
completed this step of the construction and the recursion may continue.

Since we can construct $b_0, \dots, b_n$ satisfying (a) and (b) for any $n$,
we have $\limsup N(x)/x \leq 1/2^n$ for any $n$, yielding $\lim N(x)/x =
0$ as desired.

\textbf{Second solution:} (by Paul Pollack)
Let $S$ be the set of possible values of $\limsup N(x)/x$;
since $S \subseteq [0,1]$ is bounded, it has a least upper bound $L$.
Suppose by way of contradiction that $L>0$;
we can then choose $\calA, \calB$ satisfying the conditions of the problem such
that $\limsup N(x)/x > 3L/4$.

To begin with, we can
certainly find some positive integer $m \notin \calB$, so that
$\calA$ is disjoint from $\calA + m = \{a +m: a \in \calA\}$.
Put $\calA' = \calA \cup (\calA + m)$ and let
$N'(x)$ be the size of $\calA' \cap \{1, \dots, x\}$; then
$\limsup N'(x)/x = 3L/2 > L$, so $\calA'$ cannot obey the conditions
of the problem statement. That is, if we let $\calB'$ be the set of positive
integers that occur as differences between elements of $\calA'$,
then there exists an integer $n$ such that among any $n$ consecutive
integers, at least one lies in $\calB'$.
But
\[
\calB' \subseteq \{b + em: b \in \calB, e \in \{-1,0,1\}\},
\]
so among any $n+2m$ consecutive integers, at least one lies in $\calB$.
This contradicts the condition of the problem statement.

We conclude that it is impossible to have $L>0$, so $L = 0$
and $\lim N(x)/x = 0$ as desired.

\textbf{Remark:} A hybrid between these two arguments is to
note that if we can produce $c_1, \dots, c_n$ such that
$|c_i - c_j| \notin \calB$ for $i,j = 1, \dots, n$, then
the translates $\calA + c_1, \dots, \calA + c_n$ are disjoint
and so $\limsup N(x)/x \leq 1/n$. Given $c_1 \leq \dots \leq c_n$
as above, we can then choose $c_{n+1}$ to be the largest element of a
run of $c_n+1$ consecutive integers, none of which lie in $\calB$.

\end{itemize}


\end{document}

descriptive_long.question:
Let $\mathcal{setalpha}$
be a non-empty set of positive integers, and let $\countn(\coordxval)$ denote
the number of elements of $\mathcal{setalpha}$ not exceeding $\coordxval$.
Let $\mathcal{setbeta}$ denote the set
of positive integers $\elementb$ that can be written in the form $\elementb = \elementa - \elementa'$ with
$\elementa \in \mathcal{setalpha}$  and $\elementa' \in  \mathcal{setalpha}$. Let $\elementbone < \elementbtwo < \cdots$
be the members of $\mathcal{setbeta}$,
listed in increasing order. Show that if the sequence $\elementbnext - \elementbindex$ is
unbounded, then
\[
\lim_{\coordxval \to\infty}  \countn(\coordxval)/\coordxval  = 0.
\]
descriptive_long.solution:
\textbf{First solution:}
(based on a solution of Dan Bernstein)
Note that for any $\elementb$, the condition that $\elementb \notin \calsetbeta$ already
forces $\limsup \countn(\coordxval)/\coordxval$ to be at most 1/2: pair off $2\indexm\elementb + \indexn$ with
$(2\indexm+1)\elementb+\indexn$ for $\indexn=1,\dots, \elementb$, and note that at most one member of each
pair may belong to $\calsetalpha$.
The idea of the proof is to do something
similar with pairs replaced by larger clumps, using long runs of
excluded elements of $\setbeta$.

Suppose we have positive integers
$\elementbzero = 1, \elementbone, \dots, \elementblast$ with
the following properties:
\begin{enumerate}
\item[(a)] For $\indexi=1, \dots, \indexn$,
$\coefficientci = \elementbindex/(2\elementbprev)$ is an integer.
\item[(b)] For $\epsilonindex \in \{-1,0,1\}$,
$|\epsilonindex_1\elementbone + \cdots + \epsilonindex \elementblast| \notin \calsetbeta$.
\end{enumerate}
Each nonnegative integer $\elementa$ has a unique ``base expansion''
\[
\elementa = \elementazero \elementbzero + \cdots + \elementaprev b_{n-1} + \indexm \elementblast \qquad (0 \leq \elementaindex < 2\coefficientci);
\]
if two integers have expansions with the same value of $\indexm$, and values
of $\elementaindex$ differing by at most 1 for $\indexi=0, \dots, \indexn-1$, then their
difference is not in $\calsetbeta$, so at most one of them lies in $\calsetalpha$.
In particular,
for any $\digitdindex \in \{0, \dots, \coefficientci - 1\}$, any $\indexmzero \in \{0, 2c_0 - 1\}$
and any $\indexmlast$, the set
\begin{multline*}
\{\indexmzero \elementbzero + (2d_1 +e_1)\elementbzero + \cdots \\
+ (2d_{n-1} + e_{n-1})b_{n-1} + (2\indexmlast + e_n)\elementblast\},
\end{multline*}
where each $\epsilonindex$ runs over $\{0,1\}$,
contains at most one element of $\calsetalpha$; consequently,
$\limsup \countn(\coordxval)/\coordxval \leq 1/2^{\indexn}$.

We now produce such $\elementbindex$ recursively, starting with $\elementbzero = 1$ (and both
(a) and (b) holding vacuously).  Given $\elementbzero, \dots, \elementblast$ satisfying (a)
and (b), note that $\elementbzero + \cdots + b_{n-1} < \elementblast$ by induction on $\indexn$.
By the hypotheses of the problem, we can find a set $\setsequence$ of $6\elementblast$
consecutive integers, none of which belongs to $\calsetbeta$. Let $\elementbafter$
be the second-smallest multiple of $2\elementblast$ in $\setsequence$; then $\elementbafter + \coordxval \in
\setsequence$ for $-2\elementblast \leq \coordxval \leq 0$ clearly, and also for $0 \leq \coordxval \leq 2\elementblast$
because there are most $4\elementblast-1$ elements of $\setsequence$ preceding $\elementbafter$.
In particular, the analogue of (b) with $\indexn$ replaced by $\indexn+1$ holds for
$e_{\indexn+1} \neq 0$; of course it holds for $e_{\indexn+1} = 0$ because (b) was
already known. Since the analogue of (a) holds by construction, we have
completed this step of the construction and the recursion may continue.

Since we can construct $\elementbzero, \dots, \elementblast$ satisfying (a) and (b) for any $\indexn$,
we have $\limsup \countn(\coordxval)/\coordxval \leq 1/2^{\indexn}$ for any $\indexn$, yielding $\lim \countn(\coordxval)/\coordxval =
0$ as desired.

\textbf{Second solution:} (by Paul Pollack)
Let $S$ be the set of possible values of $\limsup \countn(\coordxval)/\coordxval$;
since $S \subseteq [0,1]$ is bounded, it has a least upper bound $\limitl$.
Suppose by way of contradiction that $\limitl>0$;
we can then choose $\calsetalpha, \calsetbeta$ satisfying the conditions of the problem such
that $\limsup \countn(\coordxval)/\coordxval > 3\limitl/4$.

To begin with, we can
certainly find some positive integer $\indexm \notin \calsetbeta$, so that
$\calsetalpha$ is disjoint from $\calsetalpha + \indexm = \{\elementa +\indexm: \elementa \in \calsetalpha\}$.
Put $\calsetalpha' = \calsetalpha \cup (\calsetalpha + \indexm)$ and let
$\countn'(\coordxval)$ be the size of $\calsetalpha' \cap \{1, \dots, \coordxval\}$; then
$\limsup \countn'(\coordxval)/\coordxval = 3\limitl/2 > \limitl$, so $\calsetalpha'$ cannot obey the conditions
of the problem statement. That is, if we let $\calsetbeta'$ be the set of positive
integers that occur as differences between elements of $\calsetalpha'$,
then there exists an integer $\indexn$ such that among any $\indexn$ consecutive
integers, at least one lies in $\calsetbeta'$.
But
\[
\calsetbeta' \subseteq \{\elementb + e\indexm: \elementb \in \calsetbeta, e \in \{-1,0,1\}\},
\]
so among any $\indexn+2\indexm$ consecutive integers, at least one lies in $\calsetbeta$.
This contradicts the condition of the problem statement.

We conclude that it is impossible to have $\limitl>0$, so $\limitl = 0$
and $\lim \countn(\coordxval)/\coordxval = 0$ as desired.

\textbf{Remark:} A hybrid between these two arguments is to
note that if we can produce $\coefficientone, \dots, c_{n}$ such that
$|\coefficientci - c_{j}| \notin \calsetbeta$ for $\indexi,j = 1, \dots, \indexn$, then
the translates $\calsetalpha + \coefficientone, \dots, \calsetalpha + c_{n}$ are disjoint
and so $\limsup \countn(\coordxval)/\coordxval \leq 1/\indexn$. Given $\coefficientone \leq \dots \leq c_{n}$
as above, we can then choose $\coefficientafter$ to be the largest element of a
run of $c_{n}+1$ consecutive integers, none of which lie in $\calsetbeta$.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Let $\mathcal{frostwhale}$
be a non-empty set of positive integers, and let $meadowlark(sandstone)$ denote
the number of elements of $\mathcal{frostwhale}$ not exceeding $sandstone$.
Let $\mathcal{thornapple}$ denote the set
of positive integers moonlight that can be written in the form moonlight = tangerine - tangerine' with
tangerine \in \mathcal{frostwhale}  and tangerine' \in  \mathcal{frostwhale}. Let moonlight_1 < moonlight_2 < \cdots
be the members of $\mathcal{thornapple}$,
listed in increasing order. Show that if the sequence moonlight_{driftwood+1} - moonlight_{driftwood} is
unbounded, then
\[
\lim_{sandstone \to\infty}  meadowlark(sandstone)/sandstone  = 0.
\]

\end{itemize}
\end{document}
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
\textbf{First solution:}
(based on a solution of Dan Bernstein)
Note that for any moonlight, the condition that moonlight \notin \calthornapple already
forces \limsup meadowlark(sandstone)/sandstone to be at most 1/2: pair off $2m moonlight + nightowl$ with
$(2m+1)moonlight+nightowl$ for $nightowl=1,\dots, moonlight$, and note that at most one member of each
pair may belong to \calfrostwhale.
The idea of the proof is to do something
similar with pairs replaced by larger clumps, using long runs of
excluded elements of thornapple.

Suppose we have positive integers
cloudberry_0 = 1, cloudberry_1, \dots, cloudberry_brookside with
the following properties:
\begin{enumerate}
\item[(a)] For driftwood=1, \dots, brookside,
 copperleaf_{driftwood} = cloudberry_{driftwood}/(2cloudberry_{driftwood-1}) is an integer.
\item[(b)] For nightowl_{driftwood} \in \{-1,0,1\},
$|nightowl_1 cloudberry_1 + \cdots + nightowl_brookside cloudberry_brookside| \notin \calthornapple$.
\end{enumerate}
Each nonnegative integer tangerine has a unique ``base expansion''
\[
tangerine = porchlamp greenfield + \cdots + blueguitar_{brookside-1} cloudberry_{brookside-1} + glimmering cloudberry_{brookside} \qquad (0 \leq porchlamp < 2copperleaf_{1});
\]
if two integers have expansions with the same value of glimmering, and values
of blueguitar_{driftwood} differing by at most 1 for driftwood=0, \dots, brookside-1, then their
difference is not in \calthornapple, so at most one of them lies in \calfrostwhale.
In particular,
for any sunlitpath_{driftwood} \in \{0, \dots, copperleaf_{driftwood} - 1\}$, any whispering \in \{0, 2copperleaf_{0} - 1\}$
and any lanternlit, the set
\begin{multline*}
\{whispering greenfield + (2sunlitpath_1 +nightowl_1)greenfield + \cdots \\
+ (2sunlitpath_{brookside-1} + nightowl_{brookside-1})cloudberry_{brookside-1} + (2lanternlit + nightowl_{brookside})cloudberry_{brookside}\},
\end{multline*}
where each nightowl_{driftwood} runs over \{0,1\},
contains at most one element of \calfrostwhale; consequently,
$\limsup meadowlark(sandstone)/sandstone \leq 1/2^{brookside}$.

We now produce such cloudberry_{driftwood} recursively, starting with greenfield = 1 (and both
(a) and (b) holding vacuously).  Given greenfield, \dots, cloudberry_{brookside} satisfying (a)
and (b), note that greenfield + \cdots + cloudberry_{brookside-1} < cloudberry_{brookside} by induction on brookside.
By the hypotheses of the problem, we can find a set starlitbay_{brookside} of $6cloudberry_{brookside}$
consecutive integers, none of which belongs to thornapple. Let cloudberry_{brookside+1}
be the second-smallest multiple of $2cloudberry_{brookside}$ in starlitbay_{brookside}; then cloudberry_{brookside+1} + sandstone \in
starlitbay_{brookside} for $-2cloudberry_{brookside} \leq sandstone \leq 0$ clearly, and also for $0 \leq sandstone \leq 2cloudberry_{brookside}$
because there are most $4cloudberry_{brookside}-1$ elements of starlitbay_{brookside} preceding cloudberry_{brookside+1}$.
In particular, the analogue of (b) with brookside replaced by brookside+1 holds for
nightowl_{brookside+1} \neq 0$; of course it holds for nightowl_{brookside+1} = 0$ because (b) was
already known. Since the analogue of (a) holds by construction, we have
completed this step of the construction and the recursion may continue.

Since we can construct greenfield, \dots, cloudberry_{brookside} satisfying (a) and (b) for any brookside,
we have $\limsup meadowlark(sandstone)/sandstone \leq 1/2^{brookside}$ for any brookside, yielding $\lim meadowlark(sandstone)/sandstone =
0$ as desired.

\textbf{Second solution:} (by Paul Pollack)
Let $S$ be the set of possible values of $\limsup meadowlark(sandstone)/sandstone$;
since $S \subseteq [0,1]$ is bounded, it has a least upper bound marigolds.
Suppose by way of contradiction that marigolds>0$;
we can then choose \calfrostwhale, \calthornapple satisfying the conditions of the problem such
that $\limsup meadowlark(sandstone)/sandstone > 3marigolds/4$.

To begin with, we can
certainly find some positive integer glimmering \notin \calthornapple$, so that
\calfrostwhale$ is disjoint from \calfrostwhale + glimmering = \{tangerine +glimmering: tangerine \in \calfrostwhale\}$.
Put \calfrostwhale' = \calfrostwhale \cup (\calfrostwhale + glimmering)$ and let
meadowlark'(sandstone)$ be the size of \calfrostwhale' \cap \{1, \dots, sandstone\}$; then
$\limsup meadowlark'(sandstone)/sandstone = 3marigolds/2 > marigolds$, so \calfrostwhale'$ cannot obey the conditions
of the problem statement. That is, if we let \calthornapple'$ be the set of positive
integers that occur as differences between elements of \calfrostwhale'$, then there exists an integer brookside$ such that among any brookside$ consecutive
integers, at least one lies in \calthornapple'.$
But
\[
\calthornapple' \subseteq \{moonlight + nightowl glimmering: moonlight \in \calthornapple, nightowl \in \{-1,0,1\}\},
\]
so among any brookside+2glimmering$ consecutive integers, at least one lies in \calthornapple.$
This contradicts the condition of the problem statement.

We conclude that it is impossible to have marigolds>0$, so marigolds = 0$
and $\lim meadowlark(sandstone)/sandstone = 0$ as desired.

\textbf{Remark:} A hybrid between these two arguments is to
note that if we can produce copperleaf_1, \dots, copperleaf_brookside$ such that
$|copperleaf_{driftwood} - copperleaf_{hillcrest}| \notin \calthornapple$ for driftwood,hillcrest = 1, \dots, brookside$, then
the translates \calfrostwhale + copperleaf_1, \dots, \calfrostwhale + copperleaf_brookside$ are disjoint
and so $\limsup meadowlark(sandstone)/sandstone \leq 1/brookside$. Given copperleaf_1 \leq \dots \leq copperleaf_brookside$
as above, we can then choose copperleaf_{brookside+1}$ to be the largest element of a
run of copperleaf_brookside+1$ consecutive integers, none of which lie in \calthornapple$.

\end{itemize}


\end{document}

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Let $\mathcal{emptyset}$
be a non-empty set of positive integers, and let $emptymap(limitless)$ denote
the number of elements of $\mathcal{emptyset}$ not exceeding $limitless$.
Let $\mathcal{additions}$ denote the set
of positive integers $alphavar$ that can be written in the form $alphavar = omegaelem - omegaelem'$ with
$omegaelem \in \mathcal{emptyset}$  and $omegaelem' \in  \mathcal{emptyset}$. Let $alphafirst < alphasecnd < \cdots$
be the members of $\mathcal{additions}$,
listed in increasing order. Show that if the sequence $alphanext - alphagen$ is
unbounded, then
\[
\lim_{limitless \to\infty}  emptymap(limitless)/limitless  = 0.
\]
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
\textbf{First solution:}
(based on a solution of Dan Bernstein)
Note that for any $alphavar$, the condition that $alphavar \notin \caladditions$ already
forces $\limsup emptymap(limitless)/limitless$ to be at most 1/2: pair off $2staticelem alphavar + tinyindex$ with
$(2staticelem+1)alphavar+tinyindex$ for $tinyindex=1,\dots, alphavar$, and note that at most one member of each
pair may belong to $\calemptyset$.
The idea of the proof is to do something
similar with pairs replaced by larger clumps, using long runs of
excluded elements of $additions$.

Suppose we have positive integers
$alphabase = 1, alphafirst, \dots, alphatop$ with
the following properties:
\begin{enumerate}
\item[(a)] For $zerovario=1, \dots, tinyindex$,
$gammadigi = alphagen/(2alphaprev)$ is an integer.
\item[(b)] For $epsilonvar \in \{-1,0,1\}$,
$|epsilonvar_1 alphafirst + \cdots + epsilonvar_{tinyindex} alphatop| \notin \caladditions$.
\end{enumerate}
Each nonnegative integer $omegaelem$ has a unique ``base expansion''
\[
omegaelem = omegazero alphabase + \cdots + omegaprev alphaprev + staticelem alphatop \qquad (0 \leq omegacoef < 2gammadigi);
\]
if two integers have expansions with the same value of $staticelem$, and values
of $omegacoef$ differing by at most 1 for $zerovario=0, \dots, tinyindex-1$, then their
difference is not in $\caladditions$, so at most one of them lies in $\calemptyset$.
In particular,
for any $deltagen \in \{0, \dots, gammadigi - 1\}$, any $staticzero \in \{0, 2gammafirst - 1\}$
and any $statictop$, the set
\begin{multline*}
\{staticzero alphabase + (2deltagen_1 +epsilonvar_1)alphabase + \cdots \\
+ (2deltagen_{tinyindex-1} + epsilonvar_{tinyindex-1})alphaprev + (2statictop + epsilonvar_{tinyindex})alphatop\},
\end{multline*}
where each $epsilonvar$ runs over $\{0,1\}$,
contains at most one element of $\calemptyset$; consequently,
$\limsup emptymap(limitless)/limitless \leq 1/2^{tinyindex}$.

We now produce such $alphagen$ recursively, starting with $alphabase = 1$ (and both
(a) and (b) holding vacuously).  Given $alphabase, \dots, alphatop$ satisfying (a)
and (b), note that $alphabase + \cdots + alphaprev < alphatop$ by induction on $tinyindex$.
By the hypotheses of the problem, we can find a set $voidblock$ of $6alphatop$
consecutive integers, none of which belongs to $\caladditions$. Let $alphasucc$
be the second-smallest multiple of $2alphatop$ in $voidblock$; then $alphasucc + limitless \in
voidblock$ for $-2alphatop \leq limitless \leq 0$ clearly, and also for $0 \leq limitless \leq 2alphatop$
because there are most $4alphatop-1$ elements of $voidblock$ preceding $alphasucc$.
In particular, the analogue of (b) with $tinyindex$ replaced by $tinyindex+1$ holds for
$epsilonvar_{tinyindex+1} \neq 0$; of course it holds for $epsilonvar_{tinyindex+1} = 0$ because (b) was
already known. Since the analogue of (a) holds by construction, we have
completed this step of the construction and the recursion may continue.

Since we can construct $alphabase, \dots, alphatop$ satisfying (a) and (b) for any $tinyindex$,
we have $\limsup emptymap(limitless)/limitless \leq 1/2^{tinyindex}$ for any $tinyindex$, yielding $\lim emptymap(limitless)/limitless =
0$ as desired.

\textbf{Second solution:} (by Paul Pollack)
Let $S$ be the set of possible values of $\limsup emptymap(limitless)/limitless$;
since $S \subseteq [0,1]$ is bounded, it has a least upper bound $lowerlim$.
Suppose by way of contradiction that $lowerlim>0$;
we can then choose $\calemptyset, \caladditions$ satisfying the conditions of the problem such
that $\limsup emptymap(limitless)/limitless > 3lowerlim/4$.

To begin with, we can
certainly find some positive integer $staticelem \notin \caladditions$, so that
$\calemptyset$ is disjoint from $\calemptyset + staticelem = \{omegaelem +staticelem: omegaelem \in \calemptyset\}$.
Put $\calemptyset' = \calemptyset \cup (\calemptyset + staticelem)$ and let
$emptymap'(limitless)$ be the size of $\calemptyset' \cap \{1, \dots, limitless\}$; then
$\limsup emptymap'(limitless)/limitless = 3lowerlim/2 > lowerlim$, so $\calemptyset'$ cannot obey the conditions
of the problem statement. That is, if we let $\caladditions'$ be the set of positive
integers that occur as differences between elements of $\calemptyset'$,
then there exists an integer $tinyindex$ such that among any $tinyindex$ consecutive
integers, at least one lies in $\caladditions'$.
But
\[
\caladditions' \subseteq \{alphavar + epsilonvar staticelem: alphavar \in \caladditions, e \in \{-1,0,1\}\},
\]
so among any $tinyindex+2staticelem$ consecutive integers, at least one lies in $\caladditions$.
This contradicts the condition of the problem statement.

We conclude that it is impossible to have $lowerlim>0$, so $lowerlim = 0$
and $\lim emptymap(limitless)/limitless = 0$ as desired.

\textbf{Remark:} A hybrid between these two arguments is to
note that if we can produce $gammafirst, \dots, gammadigi$ such that
$|gammadigi - gammadigi| \notin \caladditions$ for $i,j = 1, \dots, tinyindex$, then
the translates $\calemptyset + gammafirst, \dots, \calemptyset + gammadigi$ are disjoint
and so $\limsup emptymap(limitless)/limitless \leq 1/tinyindex$. Given $gammafirst \leq \dots \leq gammadigi$
as above, we can then choose $gammansucc$ to be the largest element of a
run of $gammadigi+1$ consecutive integers, none of which lie in $\caladditions$.

garbled_string.question:
Let $\mathcal{A}$
be a non-empty set of positive integers, and let $fmgxavul(qzxwvtnp)$ denote
the number of elements of $\mathcal{A}$ not exceeding $qzxwvtnp$.
Let $\mathcal{B}$ denote the set
of positive integers $lfhqznco$ that can be written in the form $lfhqznco = hjgrksla - hjgrksla'$ with
$hjgrksla \in \mathcal{A}$  and $hjgrksla' \in  \mathcal{A}$. Let $uzvtgqke < nyhslvwo < \cdots$
be the members of $\mathcal{B}$,
listed in increasing order. Show that if the sequence $wavjkpto - tjrqmavb$ is
unbounded, then
\[
\lim_{qzxwvtnp \to\infty}  fmgxavul(qzxwvtnp)/qzxwvtnp  = 0.
\]
garbled_string.solution:
\textbf{First solution:}
(based on a solution of Dan Bernstein)
Note that for any $lfhqznco$, the condition that $lfhqznco \notin \calB$ already
forces $\limsup fmgxavul(qzxwvtnp)/qzxwvtnp$ to be at most 1/2: pair off $2wvnrasjo lfhqznco + kejsdavn$ with
$(2wvnrasjo+1)lfhqznco+kejsdavn$ for $kejsdavn=1,\dots, lfhqznco$, and note that at most one member of each
pair may belong to $\calA$.
The idea of the proof is to do something
similar with pairs replaced by larger clumps, using long runs of
excluded elements of $xvalerdp$.

Suppose we have positive integers
$rmpxagfu = 1, uzvtgqke, \dots, dgmehutb$ with
the following properties:
\begin{enumerate}
\item[(a)] For $bczdfqgv=1, \dots, kejsdavn$,
$voseklqn = tjrqmavb/(2xysdcvle)$ is an integer.
\item[(b)] For $praqsmle_{bczdfqgv} \in \{-1,0,1\}$,
$|praqsmle_1 uzvtgqke + \cdots + praqsmle_{kejsdavn} dgmehutb| \notin \calB$.
\end{enumerate}
Each nonnegative integer $hjgrksla$ has a unique ``base expansion''
\[
hjgrksla = pcvldmfr rmpxagfu + \cdots + mkqdrsve xysdcvle + wvnrasjo dgmehutb \qquad (0 \leq sngchtlb < 2voseklqn);
\]
if two integers have expansions with the same value of $wvnrasjo$, and values
of $sngchtlb$ differing by at most 1 for $bczdfqgv=0, \dots, kejsdavn-1$, then their
difference is not in $\calB$, so at most one of them lies in $\calA$.
In particular,
for any $kmrtbqsa \in \{0, \dots, voseklqn - 1\}$, any $ysjntpke \in \{0, 2c_0 - 1\}$
and any $qudpbfza$, the set
\begin{multline*}
\{ysjntpke rmpxagfu + (2kmrtbqsa +praqsmle_1)rmpxagfu + \cdots \\
+ (2kmrtbqsa_{kejsdavn-1} + praqsmle_{kejsdavn-1})b_{kejsdavn-1} + (2qudpbfza + praqsmle_{kejsdavn})dgmehutb\},
\end{multline*}
where each $praqsmle_{bczdfqgv}$ runs over $\{0,1\}$,
contains at most one element of $\calA$; consequently,
$\limsup fmgxavul(qzxwvtnp)/qzxwvtnp \leq 1/2^{kejsdavn}$.

We now produce such $tjrqmavb$ recursively, starting with $rmpxagfu = 1$ (and both
(a) and (b) holding vacuously).  Given $rmpxagfu, \dots, dgmehutb$ satisfying (a)
and (b), note that $rmpxagfu + \cdots + b_{kejsdavn-1} < dgmehutb$ by induction on $kejsdavn$.
By the hypotheses of the problem, we can find a set $dpscnqwv$ of $6dgmehutb$
consecutive integers, none of which belongs to $\calB$. Let $ftrdkezs$
be the second-smallest multiple of $2dgmehutb$ in $dpscnqwv$; then $ftrdkezs + qzxwvtnp \in
dpscnqwv$ for $-2dgmehutb \leq qzxwvtnp \leq 0$ clearly, and also for $0 \leq qzxwvtnp \leq 2dgmehutb$
because there are most $4dgmehutb-1$ elements of $dpscnqwv$ preceding $ftrdkezs$.
In particular, the analogue of (b) with $kejsdavn$ replaced by $kejsdavn+1$ holds for
$praqsmle_{kejsdavn+1} \neq 0$; of course it holds for $praqsmle_{kejsdavn+1} = 0$ because (b) was
already known. Since the analogue of (a) holds by construction, we have
completed this step of the construction and the recursion may continue.

Since we can construct $rmpxagfu, \dots, dgmehutb$ satisfying (a) and (b) for any $kejsdavn$,
we have $\limsup fmgxavul(qzxwvtnp)/qzxwvtnp \leq 1/2^{kejsdavn}$ for any $kejsdavn$, yielding $\lim fmgxavul(qzxwvtnp)/qzxwvtnp =
0$ as desired.

\textbf{Second solution:} (by Paul Pollack)
Let $S$ be the set of possible values of $\limsup fmgxavul(qzxwvtnp)/qzxwvtnp$;
since $S \subseteq [0,1]$ is bounded, it has a least upper bound $gchmsdkw$.
Suppose by way of contradiction that $gchmsdkw>0$;
we can then choose $\calA, \calB$ satisfying the conditions of the problem such
that $\limsup fmgxavul(qzxwvtnp)/qzxwvtnp > 3gchmsdkw/4$.

To begin with, we can
certainly find some positive integer $wvnrasjo \notin \calB$, so that
$\calA$ is disjoint from $\calA + wvnrasjo = \{hjgrksla +wvnrasjo: hjgrksla \in \calA\}$.
Put $\calA' = \calA \cup (\calA + wvnrasjo)$ and let
fmgxavul'(qzxwvtnp) be the size of $\calA' \cap \{1, \dots, qzxwvtnp\}$; then
$\limsup fmgxavul'(qzxwvtnp)/qzxwvtnp = 3gchmsdkw/2 > gchmsdkw$, so $\calA'$ cannot obey the conditions
of the problem statement. That is, if we let $\calB'$ be the set of positive
integers that occur as differences between elements of $\calA'$, then there exists an integer $kejsdavn$ such that among any $kejsdavn$ consecutive
integers, at least one lies in $\calB'$.
But
\[
\calB' \subseteq \{lfhqznco + e wvnrasjo: lfhqznco \in \calB, e \in \{-1,0,1\}\},
\]
so among any $kejsdavn+2wvnrasjo$ consecutive integers, at least one lies in $\calB$.
This contradicts the condition of the problem statement.

We conclude that it is impossible to have $gchmsdkw>0$, so $gchmsdkw = 0$
and $\lim fmgxavul(qzxwvtnp)/qzxwvtnp = 0$ as desired.

\textbf{Remark:} A hybrid between these two arguments is to
note that if we can produce $gyqnwklp, \dots, c_{kejsdavn}$ such that
$|voseklqn - c_{hlmxgrta}| \notin \calB$ for $bczdfqgv,hlmxgrta = 1, \dots, kejsdavn$, then
the translates $\calA + gyqnwklp, \dots, \calA + c_{kejsdavn}$ are disjoint
and so $\limsup fmgxavul(qzxwvtnp)/qzxwvtnp \leq 1/kejsdavn$. Given $gyqnwklp \leq \dots \leq c_{kejsdavn}$
as above, we can then choose jqheflzt to be the largest element of a
run of $c_{kejsdavn}+1$ consecutive integers, none of which lie in $\calB$.

kernel_variant.question:
Fix an integer $d\ge 2$ and write $\mathbb N=\{1,2,3,\dots\}$.
For $n=(n_1,\dots ,n_d)\in\mathbb Z^{d}$ put
\[
|n|_{\infty}:=\max_{1\le i\le d}|n_i|.
\]

For a non-empty subset
\[
\mathcal A\subset\mathbb N^{d}:=\bigl\{(n_1,\dots ,n_d):n_i\in\mathbb N\bigr\}
\]
define, for $x\ge 1$,
\[
N(x):=\bigl|\mathcal A\cap[1,x]^{d}\bigr|.
\]

Global difference set
\[
\Delta(\mathcal A):=\{a-a':a,a'\in\mathcal A,\;a\ne a'\}\subset\mathbb Z^{d}.
\]

For every coordinate $j\in\{1,\dots ,d\}$ put
\[
\mathcal B_{j}:=\bigl\{|a_j-a'_j|:a,a'\in\mathcal A,\;a_j\ne a'_j\bigr\}
              =(b_{j,1}<b_{j,2}<\dots)\subset\mathbb N .
\]

Simultaneous coordinate lacunarity.
Assume that there is a sequence of positive integers
\[
1=L_{1},\qquad L_{n+1}=4L_{n}\qquad(n\ge 1) \tag{$\Lambda$}
\]
such that, for every $n\ge 1$,
\[
(L_{n},2L_{n}]\;\cap\;\mathcal B_{j}=\varnothing\qquad(j=1,\dots ,d).\tag{$\diamondsuit_{n}$}
\]
In words:  for each $n$ the interval $(L_{n},2L_{n}]$ is simultaneously free of
one-dimensional differences along every coordinate direction.

Problem. 
Show that the counting function of $\mathcal A$ satisfies the power-saving
density bound
\[
N(x)\;\ll_{d}\;x^{\,d-\tfrac12}\qquad(x\ge 4),\tag{1}
\]
where the implied constant depends only on $d$.  In particular
\[
\lim_{x\to\infty}\frac{N(x)}{x^{\,d}}=0.\tag{2}
\]

The task is to deduce the exponent $\tfrac12$ in (1) from the genuinely
$d$-dimensional lacunarity \((\diamondsuit_{n})\) together with the uniform
radix-$4$ growth \((\Lambda)\).

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout $c,c_{1},c_{2},\dots$ denote positive constants depending only
on the ambient dimension $d$.

Step 1.  Choosing ``digit'' vectors strictly inside the forbidden gaps.  
Fix once and for all the constant
\[
\alpha:=\frac54\quad\Bigl(1<\alpha<\frac32\Bigr).
\]
For every integer $n\ge 2$ and every coordinate $j\in\{1,\dots ,d\}$ put
\[
v_{n,j}:=\Bigl\lceil\alpha\,L_{n}\Bigr\rceil\in (L_{n},2L_{n}],\qquad
v_{n}:=(v_{n,1},\dots ,v_{n,d})\in\mathbb N^{d}. \tag{3}
\]
Because $\alpha<\tfrac32$ and $L_{n}\ge 4$ for $n\ge 2$,  
\[
\alpha L_{n}<v_{n,j}\le\alpha L_{n}+1\le\frac32L_{n}. \tag{4}
\]

Step 2.  Mixed signed sums of the digit vectors avoid $\Delta(\mathcal A)$.  
For $n\ge 2$ set
\[
S_{n}:=\Bigl\{\sum_{k=2}^{n}\varepsilon_{k}v_{k}:
              \varepsilon_{k}\in\{-1,0,1\}\Bigr\}\setminus\{0\}. \tag{5}
\]
Fix $\delta=\sum_{k=2}^{n}\varepsilon_{k}v_{k}\in S_{n}$ and let
$m$ be the largest index $\ge 2$ with $\varepsilon_{m}\ne 0$.
Write
\[
\sigma_{j}:=\sum_{k=2}^{m-1}|\varepsilon_{k}|\,v_{k,j}\qquad(1\le j\le d).
\]
Using (4) and $L_{k}=4^{\,k-1}$,
\[
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{j}
 &\le \alpha\sum_{r=1}^{m-2}L_{m-r}
     =\alpha L_{m-1}\sum_{r=0}^{m-3}4^{-r}
     \le\alpha L_{m-1}\cdot\tfrac43
     =\frac{4\alpha}{3}L_{m-1}.
\end{aligned}
\]
Since $L_{m-1}=L_{m}/4$,
\[
\sigma_{j}\le\frac{\alpha}{3}L_{m}. \tag{6}
\]
With $\alpha=\tfrac54$ this gives $\sigma_{j}\le\tfrac{5}{12}L_{m}$.
Consequently
\[
\sigma_{j}\le \frac12L_{m}-1\qquad(m\ge 3),\tag{7}
\]
because $L_{m}\ge 16$ for $m\ge 3$.  
If $m=2$ then the sum for $\sigma_{j}$ is empty, hence $\sigma_{j}=0$ and
(7) is trivially satisfied as well.

Next, by (4) and (7),
\[
\begin{aligned}
|\delta_{j}|
&=|\,\varepsilon_{m}v_{m,j}\pm\sigma_{j}\,|
\;\ge\;v_{m,j}-\sigma_{j}\\
&\ge\;\alpha L_{m}-\Bigl(\tfrac12L_{m}-1\Bigr)
      \;=\;\Bigl(\alpha-\tfrac12\Bigr)L_{m}+1
      \;>\;L_{m},\tag{8}
\end{aligned}
\]
while
\[
|\delta_{j}|
\;\le\;v_{m,j}+\sigma_{j}
\;\le\;\tfrac32L_{m}+\Bigl(\tfrac12L_{m}-1\Bigr)
      \;=\;2L_{m}-1
      \;<\;2L_{m}.\tag{9}
\]
Thus for every coordinate
\[
L_{m}<|\delta_{j}|\le 2L_{m}\qquad(1\le j\le d).
\]
Each coordinate of $\delta$ therefore lies inside the lacunary interval
$(L_{m},2L_{m}]$ and,
thanks to $(\diamondsuit_{m})$, no such vector can belong
to $\Delta(\mathcal A)$.  Hence
\[
S_{n}\cap\Delta(\mathcal A)=\varnothing.\tag{Lemma}
\]

Step 3.  A large family of pairwise disjoint translates.  
For $\varepsilon=(\varepsilon_{2},\dots ,\varepsilon_{n})\in\{0,1\}^{\,n-1}$
put
\[
T(\varepsilon):=\sum_{k=2}^{n}\varepsilon_{k}v_{k}\in\mathbb N^{d}. \tag{10}
\]
If $\varepsilon\ne\eta$ then
$T(\varepsilon)-T(\eta)\in S_{n}\setminus\{0\}$; by the Lemma
\[
\bigl(\mathcal A+T(\varepsilon)\bigr)\cap
\bigl(\mathcal A+T(\eta)\bigr)=\varnothing
\qquad(\varepsilon\ne\eta).\tag{11}
\]
Thus we have $2^{\,n-1}$ pairwise disjoint translates of $\mathcal A$.

Step 4.  Localising the translates.  
Using (4) and $L_{k}=4^{\,k-1}$,
\[
\|T(\varepsilon)\|_{\infty}
 \le\alpha\sum_{k=2}^{n}L_{k}
 <\alpha L_{n}\sum_{r=0}^{n-2}4^{-r}
 <\tfrac43\alpha\,L_{n}
 <2L_{n}
 =\tfrac12L_{n+1}. \tag{12}
\]
Put $X:=L_{n+1}=4L_{n}$.  Then each translate
\[
\bigl(\mathcal A\cap[1,X]^{d}\bigr)+T(\varepsilon)
\;\subset\;[1,2X]^{d},\tag{13}
\]
and the $2^{\,n-1}$ such sets are pairwise disjoint.  Therefore
\[
2^{\,n-1}\,N(X)\;\le\;|[1,2X]^{d}|
       \;=\;(2X)^{d}
       \;=\;2^{\,d}\,X^{d}.\tag{14}
\]

Step 5.  Relating $n$ and $X$.  
Because $(\Lambda)$ is an equality,
\[
X=L_{n+1}=4^{\,n},\qquad\text{hence}\qquad
2^{\,n}=X^{1/2}.\tag{15}
\]
Consequently
\[
2^{\,n-1}=2^{-1}X^{1/2}.\tag{16}
\]
Inserting (16) into (14) gives
\[
N(X)\;\le\;2^{\,d+1}\,X^{\,d-\tfrac12}.\tag{17}
\]

Step 6.  From special abscissae to all $x$.  
The right-hand side of (17) is monotone increasing, and every
$x\ge 4$ lies in some interval $[L_{n+1},L_{n+2})$.
Using $N(x)\le N(L_{n+2})$ and (17) with $X=L_{n+2}$ yields
\[
N(x)\;\ll_{d}\;x^{\,d-\tfrac12}\qquad(x\ge 4),\tag{18}
\]
which is exactly the asserted bound (1).

Step 7.  Vanishing upper density.  
Divide (18) by $x^{d}$ and let $x\to\infty$ to obtain (2).

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1. Higher dimension.  
   • The problem is lifted from subsets of \(\mathbb N\) to subsets of
     \(\mathbb N^{d}\) with \(d\ge 2\).  
   • Density is measured in \(d\)-dimensional boxes
     \([1,x]^{d}\), so counting arguments must handle an
     extra power of \(x\).

2. Several interacting difference sets.  
   • One must control \emph{all} coordinate-wise difference sets
     \(\mathcal B_{1},\dots ,\mathcal B_{d}\) simultaneously.  
   • The hypothesis is weaker (“there exist unbounded gaps distributed
     among the coordinates’’) yet must still force sparsity.

3. Multidimensional packing.  
   • Translating the set along a \emph{vector} uses vector arithmetic
     and multidimensional volume estimates rather than simple intervals.
   • The argument must make sure that the union of many disjoint
     translates still fits into a controlled \(d\)-dimensional box.

4. Quantitative contradiction.  
   • The final inequality needs an additional power of \(x\) and an
     intricate balance between the number of translates and the volume
     of the enlarged box.  
   • Careful parameter choices (the constant \(L\), bounds on \(x\),
     etc.) are indispensable; a naïve one-dimensional adaptation fails.

All of these features force the solver to blend combinatorial packing,
coordinate projections, and asymptotic analysis in higher dimensions,
making the enhanced kernel variant markedly more technical and
substantially harder than the original one-dimensional problem.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Fix an integer $d\ge 2$ and write $\mathbb N=\{1,2,3,\dots\}$.
For $n=(n_{1},\dots ,n_{d})\in\mathbb Z^{d}$ put
\[
|n|_{\infty}:=\max_{1\le i\le d}|n_{i}|.
\]
For a non-empty subset
\[
\mathcal A\subset\mathbb N^{d}:=\bigl\{(n_{1},\dots ,n_{d}):n_{i}\in\mathbb N\bigr\}
\]
define, for $x\ge 1$,
\[
N(x):=\bigl|\mathcal A\cap[1,x]^{d}\bigr|.
\]

Global difference set
\[
\Delta(\mathcal A):=\{a-a':a,a'\in\mathcal A,\;a\ne a'\}\subset\mathbb Z^{d}.
\]

For every coordinate $j\in\{1,\dots ,d\}$ let
\[
\mathcal B_{j}:=\bigl\{|a_{j}-a'_{j}|:a,a'\in\mathcal A,\;a_{j}\ne a'_{j}\bigr\}
              =\{b_{j,1}<b_{j,2}<\dots \}\subset\mathbb N .
\]

Simultaneous coordinate lacunarity.
Assume that there is a sequence of positive integers
\[
1=L_{1},\qquad L_{n+1}=4L_{n}\qquad(n\ge 1) \tag{$\Lambda$}
\]
such that, for every $n\ge 1$,
\[
(L_{n},2L_{n}]\,\cap\,\mathcal B_{j}=\varnothing\qquad(j=1,\dots ,d).\tag{$\diamondsuit_{n}$}
\]
In words: for each $n$ the interval $(L_{n},2L_{n}]$ is simultaneously free of
one-dimensional differences along every coordinate direction.

Problem.
Prove that the counting function of $\mathcal A$ satisfies the power-saving
density bound
\[
N(x)\;\ll_{d}\;x^{\,d-\tfrac12}\qquad (x\ge 4),\tag{1}
\]
where the implied constant may depend on $d$ (on no other feature of $\mathcal A$).
In particular
\[
\lim_{x\to\infty}\frac{N(x)}{x^{\,d}}=0.\tag{2}
\]

The task is to deduce the exponent $\tfrac12$ in (1) from the genuinely
$d$-dimensional lacunarity $(\diamondsuit_{n})$ together with the uniform
``radix-$4$'' growth $(\Lambda)$.
original_kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout $c,c_{1},c_{2},\dots$ denote positive constants depending only
on the ambient dimension $d$.

Step 1.  ``Digit'' vectors inside the forbidden gaps.  
Because of $(\diamondsuit_{n})$ we may, for every $n\ge 1$ and every
$j\in\{1,\dots ,d\}$, choose an integer
\[
v_{n,j}:=\Bigl\lceil\tfrac32L_{n}\Bigr\rceil\in(L_{n},2L_{n}].\tag{3}
\]
(The ceiling is introduced only to secure integrality.)
Put
\[
v_{n}:=(v_{n,1},\dots ,v_{n,d})\in\mathbb N^{d}.\tag{4}
\]
Then for each coordinate
\[
\tfrac32L_{n}\;\le\;v_{n,j}\;<\;\tfrac32L_{n}+1\;\le\;\tfrac74L_{n}.\tag{5}
\]

Step 2.  Mixed signed sums of the digits never hit $\Delta(\mathcal A)$.  
For $n\ge 1$ set
\[
S_{n}:=\Bigl\{\sum_{k\le n}\varepsilon_{k}v_{k}:
              \varepsilon_{k}\in\{-1,0,1\}\Bigr\}\setminus\{0\}.\tag{6}
\]
Fix $\delta=\sum_{k\le n}\varepsilon_{k}v_{k}\in S_{n}$ and let
$m$ be the largest index with $\varepsilon_{m}\ne 0$.
Write
\[
\sigma_{j}:=\sum_{k<m}|\varepsilon_{k}|\,v_{k,j}\qquad(1\le j\le d).
\]
Since $L_{k}=4^{\,k-1}$,  
\[
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{j}
 &\le \tfrac32\sum_{r=1}^{m-1}L_{m-r}
   =\tfrac32L_{m-1}\sum_{r=0}^{m-2}4^{-r}
   =\tfrac32L_{m-1}\cdot\tfrac43\bigl(1-4^{-(m-1)}\bigr)  \\
 &<2L_{m-1}= \tfrac12L_{m}.\tag{7}
\end{aligned}
\]
Observe moreover that every $v_{k,j}$ is a multiple of $1$, hence
$\sigma_{j}$ is an integer.  Combining the integrality with (7) yields
\[
\sigma_{j}\;\le\;\tfrac12L_{m}-1\qquad(1\le j\le d).\tag{8}
\]

Now, by (5) and (8),
\[
|\delta_{j}|
\;=\;|\,\varepsilon_{m}v_{m,j}\pm\sigma_{j}\,|
\;\ge\;v_{m,j}-\sigma_{j}
\;\ge\;\tfrac32L_{m}-(\tfrac12L_{m}-1)
\;=\;L_{m}+1
\;>\;L_{m},\tag{9}
\]
while
\[
|\delta_{j}|
\;\le\;v_{m,j}+\sigma_{j}
\;<\;\bigl(\tfrac32L_{m}+1\bigr)+\tfrac12L_{m}
\;=\;2L_{m}+1
\;\le\;2L_{m}\qquad(m\ge 2).\tag{10}
\]
(For $m=1$ one has $L_{1}=1,\;v_{1,j}=2$, so
$|\delta_{j}|\in\{2\}\subset(L_{1},2L_{1}]$ as well.)
Thus each coordinate of $\delta$ lies in the lacunary interval
$(L_{m},2L_{m}]$; by $(\diamondsuit_{m})$ no such vector can belong
to $\Delta(\mathcal A)$.  Hence
\[
S_{n}\cap\Delta(\mathcal A)=\varnothing.\tag{Lemma 1}
\]

Step 3.  A large family of pairwise disjoint translates.  
For $\varepsilon=(\varepsilon_{1},\dots ,\varepsilon_{n})\in\{0,1\}^{n}$
put
\[
T(\varepsilon):=\sum_{k=1}^{n}\varepsilon_{k}v_{k}\in\mathbb N^{d}.\tag{11}
\]
If $\varepsilon\ne\eta$, then
$T(\varepsilon)-T(\eta)\in S_{n}\setminus\{0\}$; by Lemma 1
\[
\bigl(\mathcal A+T(\varepsilon)\bigr)\cap
\bigl(\mathcal A+T(\eta)\bigr)=\varnothing
\qquad(\varepsilon\ne\eta).\tag{12}
\]
Thus we have $2^{n}$ pairwise disjoint translates of $\mathcal A$.

Step 4.  Localising the translates.  
Using (5) and the exact radix-$4$ rule $L_{k}=4^{\,k-1}$,
\[
\|T(\varepsilon)\|_{\infty}
 \le \tfrac32\sum_{k=1}^{n}L_{k}
 =\tfrac32L_{n}\sum_{r=0}^{n-1}4^{-r}
 <2L_{n}=\tfrac12L_{n+1}.\tag{13}
\]
Put $X:=L_{n+1}$.  Then each translate
\[
\bigl(\mathcal A\cap[1,X]^{d}\bigr)+T(\varepsilon)
\;\subset\;[1,2X]^{d},\tag{14}
\]
and the $2^{n}$ such sets are pairwise disjoint.
Therefore
\[
2^{n}\,N(X)\;\le\;|[1,2X]^{d}|
       \;=\;(2X)^{d}
       \;=\;2^{\,d}\,X^{d}.\tag{15}
\]

Step 5.  Relating $n$ and $X$.  
Because $(\Lambda)$ is an equality,
\[
X=L_{n+1}=4L_{n}=4^{\,n},\qquad\text{hence}\qquad
2^{n}=X^{1/2}.\tag{16}
\]
Insert (16) into (15):
\[
N(X)\;\le\;2^{\,d}\,X^{d-\tfrac12}.\tag{17}
\]

Step 6.  From special abscissae to all $x$.  
The right-hand side of (17) is monotone increasing, and every
$x\ge 4$ lies in some interval $[L_{n+1},L_{n+2})$.
Using $N(x)\le N(L_{n+2})$ and (17) with $X=L_{n+2}$ gives
\[
N(x)\;\ll_{d}\;x^{\,d-\tfrac12}\qquad(x\ge 4),\tag{18}
\]
exactly the asserted bound (1).

Step 7.  Vanishing upper density.  
Divide (18) by $x^{d}$ and let $x\to\infty$ to obtain (2).

   • The problem is lifted from subsets of \(\mathbb N\) to subsets of
     \(\mathbb N^{d}\) with \(d\ge 2\).  
   • Density is measured in \(d\)-dimensional boxes
     \([1,x]^{d}\), so counting arguments must handle an
     extra power of \(x\).

2. Several interacting difference sets.  
   • One must control \emph{all} coordinate-wise difference sets
     \(\mathcal B_{1},\dots ,\mathcal B_{d}\) simultaneously.  
   • The hypothesis is weaker (“there exist unbounded gaps distributed
     among the coordinates’’) yet must still force sparsity.

3. Multidimensional packing.  
   • Translating the set along a \emph{vector} uses vector arithmetic
     and multidimensional volume estimates rather than simple intervals.
   • The argument must make sure that the union of many disjoint
     translates still fits into a controlled \(d\)-dimensional box.

4. Quantitative contradiction.  
   • The final inequality needs an additional power of \(x\) and an
     intricate balance between the number of translates and the volume
     of the enlarged box.  
   • Careful parameter choices (the constant \(L\), bounds on \(x\),
     etc.) are indispensable; a naïve one-dimensional adaptation fails.

All of these features force the solver to blend combinatorial packing,
coordinate projections, and asymptotic analysis in higher dimensions,
making the enhanced kernel variant markedly more technical and
substantially harder than the original one-dimensional problem.
